Because the extensive introduction of machinery in England was at first accompanied by the unhappy result of a lengthening of the hours of labor in factories, this result was deemed to be necessary in all other cases.

In all other countries of Europe and in America, where the introduction of machinery has been more gradual and normal, it has been followed immediately by a shortening of working hours, as eventually it was in England also.
Real wages are the essential thing, and as a consumer the laborer shares with every other member of society in the benefits of improved machinery.

The general, or average, gain is not to be judged by comparing the conditions of the lowest grade of society with those of fifty years ago, for while that grade may have been bettered only a little, it has been possible for large numbers to rise to higher grades because of the use of machinery.
But the crude physical labor which can compete only on the plane of automatic machines, must find its field of employment more and more hedged in.
If the wages of unskilled labor are not depressed, it is because of the enterprise of others who rise to more skilled employments and thus reduce the competitors of the lowest rank.
Whether this is its natural result is debatable, but the factory worker in general does not appear to be less intelligent than the agricultural worker.
There is a general improvement along all the lines of intelligence, morals, and health.
